title: Fundraiser

jokes.text: The inter-nerds are on it.
jokes.setup: Mike is confirming the Cornstarch Utensils online campaign is running.
jokes.punchline: He calls internet users 'inter-nerds,' a malapropism that reveals his technophobic cluelessness.
jokes.explanation: A tight, economical character joke. 'Inter-nerds' is a perfectly chosen malapropism — it sounds like something a technologically illiterate press secretary would actually say. The character integration is strong; this kind of confident ignorance is Mike's whole deal.

jokes.text: Yeah, is that a poppy seed there in your teeth?
jokes.setup: Amy instructs Selina to smile for the senators.
jokes.punchline: She immediately undercuts her own advice by pointing out Selina has something in her teeth.
jokes.explanation: The rapid pivot from pep talk to mortifying personal note is a very Veep structural move. Works because it lands right as the scene accelerates — Amy offers a confidence boost and immediately deflates it. The 'yeah, ugh' follow-up is a nice grace note.

jokes.text: Could we maybe table tableware for now, Gary, and focus on filibuster reform?
jokes.setup: Gary keeps trying to show off the Cornstarch fork and spoon while Amy wants to discuss policy.
jokes.punchline: Amy's exasperated request uses 'table tableware' — an unintentional (or possibly intentional) pun that also captures her contempt for Gary.
jokes.explanation: Elegant wordplay embedded in a character moment. 'Table tableware' is funny whether Amy intends it or not — and the ambiguity makes it smarter. The rhythm of the line is perfect Iannucci: the pun doesn't slow the walk-and-talk, it's baked into it.

jokes.text: I mean, if I can get Cornstarch Utensils in most federal buildings by the fall, well, then the VEEP has landed. That is what we are working on, landing you. Like a big, beautiful eagle.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to sell the Cornstarch initiative as a major political accomplishment.
jokes.punchline: The grandiose 'the VEEP has landed' / 'big beautiful eagle' metaphor is hilariously disproportionate to the goal of swapping plastic forks.
jokes.explanation: Works as character comedy and as a thesis statement for the whole show: Selina's grandiosity about trivial achievements. The Apollo 11 allusion applied to biodegradable cutlery is perfectly absurd, and 'big beautiful eagle' has an almost childlike quality that's endearing and sad.

jokes.text: Not yet. He's mostly intravenous. He has so many tubes in him, he looks like a set of bagpipes.
jokes.setup: Selina casually asks if Senator Reeves is dead yet.
jokes.punchline: Amy responds with cheerful clinical detail, then delivers the bagpipes simile — a grotesquely funny image delivered completely straight.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The bagpipes simile is genuinely original — it's a specific, vivid, and unexpected comparison that earns its dark laugh. What makes it exceptional is the double-layered callousness: Selina's casual 'is he dead yet,' and Amy's equally unbothered clinical response escalating into imagery. The economy is superb: one sentence, complete picture. Quotable and memorably Veep in tone.

jokes.text: Glasses on for the intellectual look? / A woman with vision. / No, glasses make me look weak. / Yeah, I agree. / It's like a wheelchair for the eye.
jokes.setup: Selina debates whether to wear glasses for the meeting.
jokes.punchline: 'It's like a wheelchair for the eye' — an unexpectedly offensive but perfectly logical analogy that reveals the character's politically incorrect casual cruelty.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed exchange that earns its payoff through the rapid volley of perspectives. 'A woman with vision' is a decent gag, but 'wheelchair for the eye' is the real prize — an original, logically consistent, spectacularly tactless metaphor. Prescient of the show's later 'retard' controversy in the same episode. Strong character integration: only someone of Selina's particular vanity and political obliviousness would say this.

jokes.text: Senator Phil Dorsey, 2:00. / I'm not a sniper.
jokes.setup: Gary points out Senator Dorsey at '2:00' using clock-position navigation.
jokes.punchline: Selina flatly declares she's not a sniper, rejecting the military-style directional cue.
jokes.explanation: Extremely economical joke — four words, big laugh. The sniper image is absurdist and perfectly specific. Works because it's delivered completely flat, treating a bizarre logic as matter-of-fact.

jokes.text: His daughter Emily just graduated from Harvard. / Tell me, how is Emily?
jokes.setup: Gary whispers background intel on Dorsey so Selina can seem personable.
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately deploys it as if she already knew — the fakeness of political schmoozing laid bare in one beat.
jokes.explanation: A well-observed political satire beat — the aide-whispering-intel trick is a real thing and the show deploys it cleanly. Not especially original but true and efficient.

jokes.text: There aren't enough people to fill a fucking canoe in here.
jokes.setup: Selina's fundraiser has almost no attendees.
jokes.punchline: She quantifies the humiliation with the 'fucking canoe' metric — a vivid, specific, and profane measure of failure.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest lines. 'A fucking canoe' is a perfectly chosen unit of measurement — small enough to be insulting, specific enough to be funny, and delivered with Selina's characteristic profane indignation. The image is vivid and the economy is exceptional. This is Iannucci insult writing at its best: original, character-driven, and punchy.

jokes.text: Senator Mike Dudley. He's interested in maps. / Mike, you found us. / Is that a map joke? / Yes, it is.
jokes.setup: Amy mentions Senator Dudley is interested in maps. Selina greets him.
jokes.punchline: Selina improvises a weak map-finding pun, Dudley calls it out directly, and Selina proudly confirms it.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully meta-structured joke: the pun is intentionally bad, the straight-man confirmation ('Is that a map joke?') is the real laugh, and Selina's proud 'yes it is' is the button. Three levels of comedy in four lines. Excellent structure.

jokes.text: This whole building is bankrolled by plastics. This is why nobody showed up.
jokes.setup: The team discovers a tweet upset the plastics industry, who fund the people in the building.
jokes.punchline: The terrible irony is revealed: they sabotaged their own event by tweeting against the industry that funds everyone they invited.
jokes.explanation: More plot revelation than joke, but the irony is sharp enough to earn a comedic beat. Functions as the episode's inciting comedic disaster.

jokes.text: Mike, talk to me. I am in a room with three people and a fuckload of quiche.
jokes.setup: Selina is panicking about the empty fundraiser.
jokes.punchline: 'A fuckload of quiche' — the contrast between profanity and the twee canapé is the joke, plus the image of a pathetically catered empty room.
jokes.explanation: Strong line. 'Fuckload of quiche' is a great juxtaposition — the vulgarity of 'fuckload' against the delicacy of quiche creates instant bathos. Economical, vivid, character-specific. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Do these not bend the fuck back? No. No. What am I supposed to do with this? I'm supposed to eat around corners? Is that how's gonna work?
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to use a Cornstarch fork — the very product she's championing.
jokes.punchline: The biodegradable fork immediately fails its most basic function in her hands, and she melts down about it.
jokes.explanation: Superb character-comedy beat. The Vice President of the United States cannot use the item she's staking her political identity on. 'Eat around corners' is a sharp specific absurdity. The physical comedy (inferrable from the dialogue) and the escalating indignation work together perfectly.

jokes.text: How do you suggest that I mingle with this few people? Did Simon mingle with Garfunkel?
jokes.setup: Amy tells Selina to mingle at the nearly-empty event.
jokes.punchline: Selina invokes Simon & Garfunkel as the reference point for impossible mingling, which makes no logical sense and therefore is exactly the kind of thing she'd say.
jokes.explanation: The Simon & Garfunkel non sequitur is wonderfully strange and very specific to Selina's age and cultural reference frame. The follow-up 'Well, they work together, they probably socialized together' from Amy grounding the absurdity makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: Well, you know what I say, it's a dirty job and I love to do it.
jokes.setup: Dan compliments the clean jobs initiative.
jokes.punchline: Selina's off-the-cuff quip misquotes/inverts the famous 'dirty job' phrase — suggesting either she's not a great spontaneous wit or she's too frantic to care.
jokes.explanation: Mild joke that works mainly as a character beat — Selina attempting wit and landing slightly flat, which is itself the joke. Not particularly sharp writing but functional.

jokes.text: Power. I'm kidding!
jokes.setup: Selina asks Hallowes what she's been missing.
jokes.punchline: Hallowes answers 'Power' — completely honestly — and then deflects with 'I'm kidding!' that convinces no one.
jokes.explanation: Sharp two-word joke with a deft button. 'Power' lands as an honest assessment of what the VP is missing; the 'I'm kidding' makes it clear she's not. Elegant and nasty.

jokes.text: Guess what? I've managed to get a fresh batch of those European sweeteners you like so much. / Oh, thanks, Gary. Yeah, they're great sweeteners. / They are awful. / Actually, they're just sweeteners I got at a NATO meeting last year.
jokes.setup: Gary proudly presents special sweeteners for the VP.
jokes.punchline: Selina goes along with them being 'great,' then Amy reveals they're awful, then Gary deflates the whole mythology by admitting they're random NATO freebies.
jokes.explanation: Three-beat escalating deflation: Selina performs gratitude, Amy speaks truth, Gary accidentally destroys his own pretension. The structure is very tight. Also introduces the Gary-as-hapless-devoted-servant dynamic economically.

jokes.text: Selina reacting to the bitter sweetener — 'They really taste rather... Uh, unpleasant. Kind of bitter.' — while maintaining polite conversation with Hallowes.
jokes.setup: Selina has just been handed sweeteners Gary is proudly promoting; she's in a political conversation and must be polite.
jokes.punchline: The slow dawning horror — spread over nearly 10 seconds of transcript gaps — as she processes how bad they taste while trying to keep up appearances.
jokes.explanation: A sustained cringe moment dependent on performance — the gaps in the transcript [04:48], [04:53], [04:57] suggest Selina taking tiny sips and grimacing while trying to maintain composure. The comedy is entirely in the gap between her public face and private suffering, a Veep specialty.

jokes.text: Not really. She's middle of the road. She's mediocre, really. Of all the -ocres, she's the mediest.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Dan if he enjoys working for Senator Hallowes.
jokes.punchline: 'Of all the -ocres, she's the mediest' — Dan deconstructs the word 'mediocre' to create a superlative that doesn't exist but lands perfectly.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best individual line. 'Of all the -ocres, she's the mediest' is a genuine feat of writing: it takes a standard insult, strips it to its morphological bones, and reassembles it into a superlative that is simultaneously ridiculous and perfectly clear. The construction is original — this doesn't feel borrowed from anywhere. It's the kind of line that makes comedy writers jealous. Comparable in wit economy to the '8.0 anchor' reference in the rubric; this edges above it for its outright originality. Dan's character — the smooth, dangerously clever operator — is fully expressed in one sentence.

jokes.text: All right, watch me, autismo. You take the little pod, put the little pod in the fucking hole, shut the fucking lid, hit the fucking button for two fucking seconds.
jokes.setup: Gary has been fumbling with a coffee machine for an embarrassingly long time.
jokes.punchline: Dan snaps and delivers furious espresso instructions with escalating profanity, climaxing in four consecutive 'fucking's.
jokes.explanation: The rhythm of the profanity escalation is masterfully written — 'put the little pod in the fucking hole, shut the fucking lid, hit the fucking button' works as a triple build. 'Autismo' as an opening address is delightfully contemptuous. Dan introducing himself as a character through condescension is efficient storytelling.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, next time pack an espresso machine in your big fuckin' bitch bag.
jokes.setup: Gary is frustrated at missing the instructions.
jokes.punchline: Dan attacks Gary's oversized bag — 'The Leviathan' — with the perfect dismissal.
jokes.explanation: 'Big fuckin' bitch bag' is excellent invective — three hard sounds in a row. The callback to the bag nickname makes it land harder. Dan's immediate cruelty on first meeting, and specifically his cruelty to Gary, establishes the show's social hierarchy instantly.

jokes.text: He ran press for Moses, didn't he?
jokes.setup: Selina mentions she still has Mike running her press shop.
jokes.punchline: Dan implies Mike is so old and ineffective he must have been doing PR since biblical times.
jokes.explanation: One of the sharpest insults in the episode. The Moses reference is perfectly specific — it's not just 'he's old,' it's 'he's so old he handled the original communications crisis.' Very economical, very quotable.

jokes.text: Dan seems nice. / He's new.
jokes.setup: Dan has just erupted at Gary with a volley of profanity and insults.
jokes.punchline: Selina observes that 'Dan seems nice' with complete deadpan, and Hallowes explains it away with 'He's new.'
jokes.explanation: A perfect deadpan exchange. 'He's new' as an explanation for spectacular rudeness is both absurd and politically believable. The two lines work as a unit and the economy is superb.

jokes.text: Oh, how nice. Thank you very much. / Two things I would have done differently.
jokes.setup: Dan compliments Selina's primaries campaign, she thanks him warmly.
jokes.punchline: He immediately pivots to critiquing it with unsolicited and specific advice, delivered without irony.
jokes.explanation: The moment perfectly illustrates Dan's dangerous self-confidence and complete social blindness (or aggressive social awareness). The structure is tight: compliment → acceptance → immediate undermining. His specific criticisms ('spent too much time in New Hampshire,' 'attack ads four days too soon') make it worse, not better. 'But, I mean, hey, you're the old pro here' is a perfectly weaponized false deference.

jokes.text: He was full of bourbon. And he grabbed my left tit. / Remember that? / And God rest his soul.
jokes.setup: Selina and Hallowes share a fond reminiscence about the late Senator Reeves.
jokes.punchline: The 'tribute' culminates in Selina casually recounting that Reeves grabbed her breast — and Hallowes responds with 'God rest his soul' as if this is a normal and slightly charming thing to memorialize.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest dark comedy beats. The warmth of the reminiscence — 'He was full of bourbon. And he grabbed my left tit' — delivered with genuine nostalgia is a perfect encapsulation of Washington's tolerance for powerful men's bad behavior. 'Remember that?' and 'God rest his soul' as the buttons normalize the assault completely. The specificity of 'left tit' (not 'breast,' not 'chest' — left tit) is perfect. Deeply Veep.

jokes.text: Hey, did you fire your tweet monkey yet? Because that guy is a weapons-grade retard. / I think you might have been hoist by your own retard there. / That's a good one. / Your own retard. Nice.
jokes.setup: Hallowes asks if Selina fired the person responsible for the problematic tweet.
jokes.punchline: Selina reworks 'hoist by your own petard' to 'hoist by your own retard' — the joke that will later become the public gaffe.
jokes.explanation: The seed of the episode's central crisis. The private joke that becomes a public disaster when Selina repeats it at the fundraiser. Dramaturgically, this is a planted-and-paid-off joke — funny here, catastrophic later. The mutual appreciation ('That's a good one. / Your own retard. Nice.') is darkly funny in retrospect.

jokes.text: Sorry about the Senate meeting. / Yeah, it was like a funeral for a homeless guy.
jokes.setup: Amy apologizes for the disastrously attended fundraiser.
jokes.punchline: Selina's simile — 'a funeral for a homeless guy' — captures both the emptiness and the sadness of the occasion perfectly.
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode. 'A funeral for a homeless guy' is original, specific, and darkly perfect — it captures the low turnout, the lack of dignity, and the general sadness of the VP's political position. The dark class-consciousness embedded in the joke (the one occasion where no one shows up is when a homeless person dies) makes it smarter than it first appears. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: 'Selina, hi. I'm melting. I'm melting.'
jokes.setup: Amy asks how the meeting with Hallowes went.
jokes.punchline: Selina's impression of Hallowes as the Wicked Witch of the West — casting her as a villain destroyed by Selina's righteousness — is petty and self-aggrandizing.
jokes.explanation: Decent character beat — the Wicked Witch reference is a bit obvious but the delivery context (Selina doing a petty impression) works. The 'She is such a fucking bitch' button is funnier for its bluntness after the dramatic impression.

jokes.text: If I genuinely don't know, then those bastards can't make me tell them. You know my motto, 'I don't want to know.' Anyways, what motto? I don't have a motto.
jokes.setup: Mike asks Selina what to say about plastics at the press call.
jokes.punchline: Selina articulates a clear policy of deliberate ignorance, calls it her motto, then immediately disavows having a motto — revealing the strategy is accidental.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character-defining comedy. Selina's plausible-deniability instinct is funny in itself, but the self-contradiction — 'that's my motto / I don't have a motto' — reveals that even her cynical political strategy is too incoherent to be deliberate. Strong character integration: this joke could only be told by a character whose self-awareness is both acute and completely absent.

jokes.text: Hey, Sue. That is a lovely dress. / Thank you, Gary.
jokes.setup: Gary tries to compliment Sue to soften a difficult interaction.
jokes.punchline: Sue accepts it graciously to Gary but gives the VP the business immediately after — her politeness to Gary and efficiency with Selina are contrasted.
jokes.explanation: A small character beat establishing Sue's no-nonsense nature. Funny but not especially crafted — mostly set-up for the 'Madam Vice President' pivot.

jokes.text: The diary's been violated.
jokes.setup: Sue is updating Selina on admin issues including a calendar conflict.
jokes.punchline: The melodramatic 'violated' for a scheduling conflict is delightfully overwrought.
jokes.explanation: A very economical laugh — two words doing the work of a whole complaint. Sue's deadpan formality makes ordinary administrative problems sound like crimes. 'Violated' is exactly the right word choice.

jokes.text: Oh, Gary. Um... Oh. Oh, my God, what was I just gonna say? Oh. I don't know. It just went out of my head.
jokes.setup: Gary hands Selina a coffee just as she's about to say something important.
jokes.punchline: The coffee disrupts her train of thought entirely; she completely loses whatever she was about to say — possibly something important — and shrugs it off.
jokes.explanation: A performance-dependent beat — the gaps in the transcript [08:43], [08:47] indicate physical comedy and facial reaction from Julia Louis-Dreyfus. Reliant on delivery but the structural joke (Gary's helpfulness backfiring) is consistent with his character.

jokes.text: I told him you were too busy to see him, but, as you see, he ignored me and, by implication, you.
jokes.setup: Jonah from the White House has shown up uninvited.
jokes.punchline: Sue's perfectly bureaucratic framing — 'by implication, you' — as if the insult to Selina is a logical corollary she's merely noting for the record.
jokes.explanation: Sue's deadpan diplomatic formulation is excellent character comedy — she's technically reporting the situation but the 'by implication, you' reveals her awareness of the political humiliation, delivered completely flatly.

jokes.text: Close the door. On your way out, close it. Quickly. Quick, quick. Quick, quick. God Almighty. No.
jokes.setup: Jonah from the White House enters uninvited.
jokes.punchline: Selina tries to shut the door on Jonah before he can get in, escalating from polite to frantic to invoking the divine — all while he presumably stands in the doorway.
jokes.explanation: The escalating panic of the repeated 'quick's works in performance — the rhythm builds nicely. 'God Almighty' as genuine supplication is a nice touch. Mostly a setup to establish Jonah's character as unwelcome.

jokes.text: You, did you check the Wikipedia? Did they change her weight again? Somebody keeps hacking into the site and changing her weight.
jokes.setup: Jonah enters the VP's office.
jokes.punchline: His first act is to ask about someone hacking Wikipedia to change the VP's weight — revealing the White House is aware of (and possibly complicit in) petty online sabotage.
jokes.explanation: An excellent Jonah introduction joke. The specificity — Wikipedia, weight, hacking — is what makes it great. It implies an entire ecosystem of White House pettiness without explaining it. The complete non sequitur delivery (no acknowledgment that this is strange) makes it weirder and funnier.

jokes.text: I love Italian, Jonah, but still, unfortunately, really dislike you.
jokes.setup: Jonah invites Amy to a new Italian restaurant.
jokes.punchline: Amy accepts the premise of Italian food being good, then rejects Jonah himself as the specific obstacle.
jokes.explanation: Very well-structured insult. The 'I love X but dislike you' construction is a classic but the delivery — 'really dislike you,' not hate, not despise, just 'dislike' — is perfectly calibrated contempt. 'Unfortunately' is the cherry on top.

jokes.text: Oh, I wasn't asking you out. I don't want you to think that was a burn because I wasn't asking you out.
jokes.setup: Amy rejects Jonah's restaurant suggestion.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's defensive backpedaling — insisting it wasn't an invitation — is transparently untrue and makes it worse.
jokes.explanation: Solid cringe beat — Jonah's attempt to reclaim dignity backfires immediately. The phrase 'I don't want you to think that was a burn' is perfectly awkward.

jokes.text: Hey, it's the guy from 'My Left Foot.'
jokes.setup: Jonah is in the room being insufferable.
jokes.punchline: Mike compares Jonah to the protagonist of 'My Left Foot' — a man with cerebral palsy — implying Jonah is uniquely ungainly or disabled in some professional sense.
jokes.explanation: Short insult that works by specificity — 'My Left Foot' is an unexpected reference and the meaning is delightfully ambiguous (is he calling him disabled? ungainly? just an insult?). Callbacks to Dan's earlier 'left foot' quip make it a small running gag.

jokes.text: Yeah, 'cause you need to put it up on that star chart on your refrigerator.
jokes.setup: Jonah brags about how many times he spoke to the President.
jokes.punchline: Amy compares his tracking of presidential contact to a child's behavior chart — treating his ambition as childish vanity.
jokes.explanation: Sharp and specific insult — the star chart on the refrigerator is a very precise image that infantilizes Jonah perfectly. Works as character comedy for both Amy (precise contempt) and Jonah (the kind of person who needs it).

jokes.text: Briefing room, N.S.C. meeting, hallway twice. / And in each of those did he say, 'Someone get this freak the fuck away from me'?
jokes.setup: Jonah lists the four occasions he spoke to the President that day.
jokes.punchline: Selina suggests the President's message in each interaction was 'get this freak the fuck away from me.'
jokes.explanation: Well-timed question that reframes all of Jonah's bragging as evidence against him. The specificity of 'this freak' landing on the word 'freak' rather than something softer is the right choice.

jokes.text: Obviously they won't ask you guys because you have no... um, you just have other areas of expertise.
jokes.setup: Jonah is explaining why the White House wants Selina to attend the fundraiser instead of the President — he needs to explain the VEEP's political irrelevance.
jokes.punchline: He starts to say she has 'no [power/credibility]' and then corrects to 'other areas of expertise' — the correction itself is the insult, because everyone in the room heard what he almost said.
jokes.explanation: A masterfully constructed cringe moment — the pause mid-sentence followed by the polite correction is more devastating than finishing the insult would have been. The audience and Selina both know what Jonah was about to say. High craft for its economy and structural precision.

jokes.text: The President of the United States of America is very keen... That your going to the fund-raiser should be fundamentally the sequence of events that does actually take place this evening.
jokes.setup: Jonah is delivering a White House order for Selina to attend a fundraiser.
jokes.punchline: He mangles the phrasing so completely that his command becomes incomprehensible bureaucratic word salad.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character-comedy moment. Jonah is delivering a command from the most powerful office in the world and the message has been so thoroughly strangled by his awkwardness that it barely parses as English. 'Fundamentally the sequence of events that does actually take place' is a master class in bureaucratic non-communication.

jokes.text: And I'd like you to watch your tone with me. / I will watch my tone.
jokes.setup: After the fundraiser is agreed to, Jonah asserts authority.
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'I will watch my tone' is a completely flat and robotic compliance that makes a mockery of his authority.
jokes.explanation: Amy's total-compliance-as-contempt is a recognizable workplace comedy bit, but it's done well here. The absolute absence of affect in 'I will watch my tone' is funny.

jokes.text: Don't tell me what to do, Doogie Fucking Howser.
jokes.setup: Jonah tells Mike not to go home to walk his dog.
jokes.punchline: Mike fires back with 'Doogie Fucking Howser' — a perfectly chosen cultural reference (young, over-earnest boy genius) applied with brilliant profane punctuation.
jokes.explanation: The insertion of 'Fucking' into 'Doogie Howser' is inspired — it takes a gentle, dated pop culture reference and weaponizes it perfectly. Very economical. The follow-up 'I don't know what that means' from Jonah is a great button, fully committing to his cluelessness.

jokes.text: That address makes me hard.
jokes.setup: Jonah announces he's going back to the White House.
jokes.punchline: He follows up with an unprompted sexual response to saying the address — 'The White House makes me hard' — treating it as a natural thing to say.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's sexual weirdness around power is well-established in the series; this is its first appearance. The non-sequitur delivery and complete absence of self-awareness make it work. 'Kiss you, miss you' as a follow-up makes it weirder.

jokes.text: What would you say were the two biggest campaign mistakes that we made? / You looked tired a lot and the hat. / I liked the hat. What are you talking about? / The hat hurt us. / Your head looked weird in the hat, that's all I'm gonna say.
jokes.setup: Selina, having just heard Dan's unsolicited campaign critiques, now asks Mike the same question.
jokes.punchline: Mike's response is devastatingly personal — not strategy, not messaging, but 'you looked tired' and 'your head looked weird in the hat.'
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke that works on multiple levels: it's a callback to Dan's sophisticated campaign critique, but Mike's version is entirely about physical appearance. 'Your head looked weird in the hat, that's all I'm gonna say' is a great button — his finality suggests there's more he could say but is choosing to be kind, which is somehow worse. Strong character integration for both: Mike's blunt simplicity and Selina's vanity collide perfectly.

jokes.text: Oh, Dan is a shit. / You want to expand on that? / Sure. He's a massive and total shit.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Mike what he thinks of Dan.
jokes.punchline: Mike calls him 'a shit,' agrees to expand, and then adds only 'massive and total' — no new information, just more emphasis.
jokes.explanation: Superb three-beat setup. The comedy is structural: 'You want to expand on that?' promises elaboration. 'He's a massive and total shit' expands the quantity but adds no quality — it's the same assessment with intensifiers. Mike's deadpan willingness to be asked and then immediately fail to deliver is perfect. The setup-request-denial structure is economical and airtight.

jokes.text: 'Cause like if there were a book with covers made of shit, you'd think, 'That's intriguing. I wonder what's in this book that they saw fit to give it covers made of pure shit.' And then you open it and... shit.
jokes.setup: Mike continues expanding on his assessment of Dan.
jokes.punchline: The extended 'book with shit covers' metaphor builds elaborate logical expectations and then collapses into the simplest possible conclusion — 'shit.'
jokes.explanation: The episode's standout joke. The 'book with shit covers' speech is a masterwork of comedic structure: it builds a sustained, internally logical metaphor that mimics how one might consider giving someone the benefit of the doubt — and then delivers the most reductive possible conclusion. The pause before the final 'shit' is the punchline within the punchline. The joke also works on a character level: Mike is too honest and too weary to elaborate in any other way. This is what an episodic standout joke looks like — quotable, brilliantly structured, character-specific, and deeply satisfying.

jokes.text: She's got a small mustache. It's a little disturbing. Don't stare at it, okay?
jokes.setup: Amy is briefing Selina on who to talk to at the fundraiser.
jokes.punchline: She casually notes Congresswoman Clements has a small mustache and instructs Selina not to stare — the warning implying she will definitely stare.
jokes.explanation: Decent visual-implication joke — we never see the mustache, it's entirely in the mind. The 'don't stare' warning is the better beat. Not especially original but efficiently done.

jokes.text: Stay away from both. The utensils are politicized. You could use celery as a kind of shovel.
jokes.setup: Selina asks about the utensil situation at the fundraiser — Cornstarch vs. plastic.
jokes.punchline: Amy's solution is to use celery as an eating implement — a piece of advice so practically absurd it underlines how completely they've painted themselves into a corner.
jokes.explanation: The celery suggestion is the absurdist peak of the Cornstarch storyline — the logical endpoint of a political crisis about utensils is 'eat with vegetables.' The deadpan delivery ('a kind of shovel') and the utter practicality of Amy's tone make it funnier than if it were played for laughs. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: I cannot believe you are dating your boss's daughter. / She's fun, she's sexy. She can advance my career. I really like her. / That's the one. / She's great. / That's the one.
jokes.setup: Amy confronts Dan about dating his boss's daughter.
jokes.punchline: Dan lists her attributes: fun, sexy, career-advancing. He adds 'I really like her' as an afterthought. Amy's deadpan 'that's the one' captures everything wrong with his priorities.
jokes.explanation: One of the best character comedy beats in the episode. Dan's complete sincerity about ranking career advancement above genuine feeling — and then tacking 'I really like her' on at the end — is his character in a nutshell. Amy's 'that's the one' (repeated) is devastating in its flat irony. The repetition after Dan says 'She's great' makes it better.

jokes.text: I'll redact your fucking face.
jokes.setup: The team informs Selina that the White House wants to heavily redact her speech.
jokes.punchline: Selina threatens to 'redact' Dan's face — applying the bureaucratic term as a physical threat.
jokes.explanation: A great verbal construction — taking a policy word and using it as violent threat. The unexpected crossover of administrative and physical violence is very Veep. Extremely economical. Very quotable.

jokes.text: This has been pencil-fucked completely? / Uh, yes, front and back. Very little romance.
jokes.setup: Selina asks if the entire speech has been gutted by the White House edits.
jokes.punchline: Amy confirms with 'front and back. Very little romance' — extending the sexual metaphor of 'pencil-fucked' to its logical conclusion.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding exchange. 'Pencil-fucked' is already a brilliant coinage — both the bureaucratic act of editing and the violence of it are captured. But Amy's 'front and back, very little romance' is the masterstroke — she plays the metaphor completely straight, extending it deadpan without breaking character. This is Iannucci's office-sex-metaphor technique at its best: the joke runs from crude invention to refined understatement in two lines. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: What's left here? I've got 'hello' and I have... Prepositions.
jokes.setup: Selina looks at her gutted speech.
jokes.punchline: The entire speech has been reduced to 'hello' and prepositions — linguistic elements that mean nothing without the words they connect.
jokes.explanation: Sharp joke. 'Prepositions' as the residue of a speech is both grammatically precise and perfectly absurd. The escalation from 'hello' down to parts of speech captures the bureaucratic annihilation of the Vice President's voice economically.

jokes.text: That's your 'Sergeant Pepper's.'
jokes.setup: Gary is trying to help Selina find something in her gutted speech to build on.
jokes.punchline: Gary compares Selina's ability to talk about filibuster reform to the Beatles' landmark album — an absurdly grandiose comparison that reveals his total devotion and poor judgment simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: The Sergeant Pepper's reference is perfect Gary: his adoration makes everything Selina does seem like a work of genius. The gap between 'Senate vote reform' and 'Sgt. Pepper's' is the comedy. Character integration is excellent.

jokes.text: We can also use my kitten heels gag. / Is that funny? / No. / It's funny enough for these people.
jokes.setup: Gary suggests a pre-prepared joke for Selina's improvised speech.
jokes.punchline: Amy is asked if the joke is funny. She says no. Gary reframes: 'It's funny enough for these people.' The condescension of 'these people' — about the audience they're trying to impress — is the punch.
jokes.explanation: Excellent three-beat joke. Amy's flat 'no' is great, but 'funny enough for these people' is the real laugh — a political operative dismissing the intelligence of the very audience they're trying to win over. Strong character work for both Gary and Amy.

jokes.text: I have stepped into the president's shoes this evening... Who knew he wore kitten heels?
jokes.setup: Selina begins her improvised speech noting she's filling in for the President.
jokes.punchline: The pre-planned 'kitten heels' joke lands — but leads to a stilettos follow-up that itself stumbles, creating cascading awkwardness.
jokes.explanation: The meta-layer — we've been told the joke isn't good, so when it lands it's both a relief and proof of Gary's low bar. The follow-up 'stilettos' ad-lib is the better joke: suggesting the male president wears women's heels is stranger and funnier.

jokes.text: No, he of course doesn't. He's more of a stilettos kind of guy.
jokes.setup: Selina has just made the kitten heels joke and then qualifies it.
jokes.punchline: She upgrades the President to stilettos — a more extreme and specifically 'dominatrix' shoe — with complete comic confidence.
jokes.explanation: The ad-lib escalation from kitten heels to stilettos is a good improvisational comedy structure — doubling down rather than retreating. The fact that she's publicly implying the President wears fetish shoes adds a political cringe layer.

jokes.text: I am reminded tonight of the late senator David Reeves. He died. He's... he's dead now.
jokes.setup: Selina attempts to pivot to a tribute to Senator Reeves, who died that day.
jokes.punchline: She lands on 'He died. He's... he's dead now' — completely unnecessary clarification of what 'late' means, delivered with escalating awkwardness.
jokes.explanation: One of the funniest public-speaking disasters in the episode. 'He died. He's... he's dead now' is the kind of thing only someone desperately stalling for time would say. The progressive tense — 'he's dead now' as if confirming an ongoing status — is wonderfully strange. The pause before 'he's dead now' makes it land much harder. Strong character integration: Selina is filling time with non-information.

jokes.text: Is your guy gonna be tweeting about this? / Okay, well, let me put it this way, Sam, that guy is no longer in a position to pay his iPhone contract. / That's because Mike McClintock there threw him into the Potomac River.
jokes.setup: Someone asks during Selina's speech whether the tweet guy will be tweeting about the event.
jokes.punchline: Selina cryptically implies he was fired. Mike then elaborates — he threw the tweet monkey into the Potomac River.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'can't pay iPhone contract' (euphemism for fired) to 'threw him in the Potomac' (apparent murder) is a good dark comedy beat. Mike's casually claiming to have committed what sounds like a murder mid-fundraiser is the best part.

jokes.text: Oh, boy. It seems with that Cornstarch tweet, we were hoist by our own retard.
jokes.setup: Selina is describing the tweet crisis at the fundraiser.
jokes.punchline: She says 'hoist by our own retard' — a mangled version of 'retard' replacing 'petard' in the Shakespearean phrase — in public, to an audience.
jokes.explanation: The word 'retard' replacing 'petard' is both a logical substitution error and a catastrophic public relations mistake. The fact that she echoes the very phrase Hallowes used privately — and deploys it publicly — is the structural joke. The callback to Hallowes' private comment makes this a planted-and-paid-off beat. The cringe of watching her say this to a room is high.

jokes.text: What if Tom Hanks dies? / What? / I'm not wishing that. I'm saying anything could happen... Tom Hanks could die, there could be a forest fire in L.A. / How did you get this job? You really want to base your press strategy based on trying to figure out when Tom Hanks is gonna...
jokes.setup: The team is in crisis mode after the 'retard' gaffe. Mike tries to suggest the story might not run if something bigger happens.
jokes.punchline: Mike invokes Tom Hanks dying as the go-to catastrophic news event that might distract the press — a very specific and absurd choice.
jokes.explanation: Tom Hanks specifically as America's most beloved-enough celebrity whose death would dominate news cycles is a very funny insight. The press strategy of 'hope Tom Hanks dies' is perfect dark satire. Amy's 'How did you get this job?' is the right button.

jokes.text: Did someone order a dick-o-gram here? / Yes, seemingly.
jokes.setup: Dan arrives in the post-speech crisis huddle uninvited.
jokes.punchline: Amy greets him as a 'dick-o-gram' — a singing telegram but for dicks — and Dan accepts the characterization flatly.
jokes.explanation: A well-coined insult. 'Dick-o-gram' is original and specific, and Dan's 'yes, seemingly' self-acceptance makes it funnier — he owns the characterization without conceding anything.

jokes.text: How did you get this job? You really want to base your press strategy on trying to figure out when Tom Hanks is gonna...
jokes.setup: Mike has proposed waiting for Tom Hanks to die as a press strategy.
jokes.punchline: Amy's incredulous 'How did you get this job?' as she trails off is a perfect reaction beat — the unfinished sentence implying the absurdity speaks for itself.
jokes.explanation: A solid reaction beat but fairly standard 'how did you get this job' incredulity comedy. The trail-off adds to it.

jokes.text: What we're gonna do is we're gonna walk slowly to the car, okay? But you guys surround me, very purposeful. Like a human motorcade. / Exactly. / We're discussing important things. Like the pope.
jokes.setup: Selina needs to exit the fundraiser with her dignity intact after the 'retard' gaffe.
jokes.punchline: The plan to look important by surrounding her 'like the pope' — comparing their ragged crisis exit to a papal procession — is magnificently grandiose.
jokes.explanation: The papal comparison is the right level of absurd grandiosity for Selina. 'Like the pope' as the benchmark for dignified entourage movement is specific and funny. The 'we're discussing important things' instruction — performative gravitas — is also good.

jokes.text: I need to keep these off your jacket 'cause the static attracts and retains dirt.
jokes.setup: The team is in the middle of a post-gaffe crisis management walk.
jokes.punchline: Gary interrupts a crisis moment to manage Selina's jacket static — his priorities are perfectly inverted. Selina's response: 'Thank you, Gary. Making physics fun.'
jokes.explanation: Excellent Gary character beat — he is obliviously focused on Selina's physical maintenance while a political crisis rages. 'Making physics fun' is a sardonic and perfectly dry response from Selina. The joke is entirely in the priority inversion.

jokes.text: Every minute that we delay, 'Retard' goes up a font size.
jokes.setup: The team is debating how to handle the 'retard' crisis.
jokes.punchline: Amy renders the escalating news cycle as font size — the bigger the story gets, the larger the headline typeface.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant media-crisis metaphor. The image of 'RETARD' increasing in headline font size with every passing minute is original, specific, and perfectly communicates the urgency. Amy's eye for politically precise dark imagery makes this one of her best lines. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Feed the dog. Walk the dog. Shoot the dog. Bury the dog. And then come back in, okay?
jokes.setup: Mike says he has to go home because of his dog. Selina tells him to handle the dog quickly.
jokes.punchline: The escalating list of dog-care instructions culminates in 'shoot the dog' and 'bury the dog' — suggesting the dog's murder as a legitimate efficiency measure.
jokes.explanation: Superb escalation joke. The sequence 'Feed, Walk, Shoot, Bury' follows the exact rhythm and tone of a to-do list — the murder of the dog is presented as simply the next logical step. The follow-up 'The dog will be fine' (presumably from Gary) after the instruction to shoot it adds a perfect absurdist button. One of the episode's top three jokes for structure and impact.

jokes.text: Front page of the style section of the 'Washington Post.' / You're the head of communications, is that correct? / Yes, ma'am. / Tuck your shirt in. Okay? Your dick is hanging out of your pants.
jokes.setup: Selina confronts Mike with the next morning's bad press about the 'retard' gaffe.
jokes.punchline: Instead of engaging with the crisis, she notices Mike's untucked shirt and deploys the 'your dick is hanging out' metaphor — calling out his unprofessionalism with deliberately extreme imagery.
jokes.explanation: Excellent multi-layered joke. The 'your dick is hanging out of your pants' applied to an untucked shirt is a brilliantly disproportionate metaphor. It also works as a commentary: Selina ignores the substantive crisis to focus on the surface presentation — which is exactly her character flaw writ small. The 'head of communications' setup is a neat structural bridge.

jokes.text: What colossal fuckup are we dealing with this morning? / Did the VEEP kill the last remaining snow leopard? / Did she firebomb a hospice?
jokes.setup: Jonah arrives the next morning sensing a crisis.
jokes.punchline: He rattles off two increasingly absurd hypothetical catastrophes — snow leopard, hospice firebombing — as casual guesses.
jokes.explanation: Two very well-chosen hypothetical disasters. 'Last remaining snow leopard' implies species extinction; 'firebombed a hospice' targets the most vulnerable. The escalation from environmental to elderly-murderous is well-calibrated. These are the kind of lines that demonstrate Iannucci's writers' room quality — they could have picked anything, and they picked the right specifics.

jokes.text: I don't have time to ignore you, Jonah. / Gary, could you please ignore Jonah for me?
jokes.setup: Jonah is making a nuisance of himself in the office the morning after the gaffe.
jokes.punchline: Selina's brilliant delegation of the task of ignoring someone — treating it as a formal professional duty she's too busy to perform herself.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest jokes. 'I don't have time to ignore you' is already a sharp paradox — acknowledging someone while claiming to ignore them. But delegating the ignoring to Gary ('could you please ignore Jonah for me?') transforms it into an absurdist masterpiece. The structural logic is airtight: if ignoring someone is a task, it can be delegated. The character integration is exceptional — only a person with Gary's specific submissive function would accept this assignment. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: You talk as if you're passing the civil rights act.
jokes.setup: Jonah delivers the condolence card with great self-importance.
jokes.punchline: Amy deflates his grandiosity by comparing his errand to the most significant congressional achievement in modern history.
jokes.explanation: Amy's contempt is perfectly calibrated here — the Civil Rights Act comparison is exactly the right level of overstatement to deflate Jonah. The economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Dickwad pie?
jokes.setup: Dan uses a casual food greeting ('Hey, good-looking, what you got cooking?') upon entering.
jokes.punchline: Amy fills in the blank with 'Dickwad pie' — treating the cooking idiom completely literally and substituting her assessment of Dan.
jokes.explanation: Sharp and economical. 'Dickwad pie' works because it plays the rhyme and food imagery of Dan's greeting completely straight, then substitutes the expected answer with a compound insult. Very efficient.

jokes.text: Shouldn't that be three? / Two is good.
jokes.setup: Selina orders two coffees for herself and Burton, her guest from the mental health charity — whose presence is directly related to her 'retard' gaffe.
jokes.punchline: Burton points out there should be three (presumably including Amy). Selina refuses him the coffee — she's already treating the mental health charity rep as a subordinate.
jokes.explanation: A subtle but enjoyable power-hierarchy joke. The VP is already pulling rank on the person she needs to appease, within seconds of meeting them. 'Two is good' is perfectly flat.

jokes.text: Uh, guys, a man is dead. / When a sexual harasser dies, we sign his wife's card. Okay? That's how Washington works.
jokes.setup: The team is casually discussing the logistics of the condolence card for the late Senator Reeves — who has already been described as a groper.
jokes.punchline: Amy delivers the operating principle of Washington political courtesy with complete deadpan moral bankruptcy.
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire. 'When a sexual harasser dies, we sign his wife's card' is a genuine insight into how Washington paper-over-everything decorum works. Jonah's earnest 'guys, a man is dead' as the setup is the perfect straight-man line. Very rewatchable.

jokes.text: That was her telling me to sign her name. / If she'd wanted me to sign your name, she would have done this... [gesture]
jokes.setup: Gary explains that Selina's gesture was an instruction to sign her name on the card.
jokes.punchline: He then demonstrates the different gesture for signing 'your name' — implying he has a whole gestural vocabulary for forgery — but the gesture evidently doesn't land on anyone.
jokes.explanation: A visual-dependent joke that works as character comedy — Gary has apparently developed an entire nonverbal code for various signature-forging scenarios, which tells you everything about the VP's office. 'That didn't get me' as a response suggests the visual gag fell flat, which is itself funny.

jokes.text: I mean, I misjudged things. Fundamentally, I would say I misjoked.
jokes.setup: Selina is reflecting on using 'retard' in her speech.
jokes.punchline: 'I misjoked' — coining a new verb by analogy with 'misspoke' that frames the problem as a failure of joke execution rather than a failure of judgment.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding coinage. 'Misjoked' is an original word that is funny on its own, but its function in Selina's moral universe is what makes it excellent — she's not saying she said something wrong, she's saying her joke misfired. The blame is on execution, not content. The 'fundamentally' adds perfect political non-apology energy. This is the kind of character-defining joke that will be remembered long after the episode. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Ha-ha! Tall, dark and tiresome, you need to get back to your boss's corner.
jokes.setup: Dan dismisses Jonah from the conversation.
jokes.punchline: 'Tall, dark and tiresome' — a parody of 'tall, dark and handsome' that replaces the attractive quality with the defining boring one.
jokes.explanation: Sharp wordplay insult. The substitution of 'tiresome' for 'handsome' is clean, specific, and memorable. Dan's casual cruelty with language is a consistent character beat throughout the episode.

jokes.text: Yeah, right after I leaked that story.
jokes.setup: Dan mentions he just resigned from working for Hallowes.
jokes.punchline: He then reveals he also immediately leaked a damaging story about her — the casual, matter-of-fact admission of betrayal is funnier than any dramatic reveal would be.
jokes.explanation: Pure character comedy. Dan revealing his betrayal of Hallowes without any dramatic fanfare — just tossing it in as an afterthought — establishes him as the most dangerous person in the room. The three-person reaction ('What? / What? / What?') is a perfect button.

jokes.text: Take a good look at me okay? Now ask yourself something, Mike. Is the man you're looking at gonna be in a position a year from now working above you or below you? 'Cause all ambition you had left your body a long time ago and now all you've got left in the last 10 remaining years of your working life is a damp apartment, cold crab cakes, and an invisible fucking dog.
jokes.setup: Dan is trying to convince Mike to go along with the Retardgate plan.
jokes.punchline: 'A damp apartment, cold crab cakes, and an invisible fucking dog' — the most specific and devastating catalogue of a middle-aged failure's life.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest speeches. 'Damp apartment, cold crab cakes, and an invisible fucking dog' is extraordinary — three nouns that together build the complete picture of a man who has quietly failed at everything, including at having a dog that anyone acknowledges. The 'invisible fucking dog' is particularly inspired: it's Mike's dog (referenced repeatedly as an obstacle to him working late), but Dan reframes it as imaginary — as if Mike's so sad that even his pet is somehow not real. The escalating specificity of the three items, each worse than the last, is masterful. Strong anchor comparison: approaches the 'snip snap' territory of personal devastating specificity.

jokes.text: In your poly-sci B.A., did you ever learn how to write a press release? / I think I might have heard of that.
jokes.setup: Dan is taking over and teaching Mike how to handle the crisis.
jokes.punchline: Mike's response — 'I think I might have heard of that' — is perfectly calibrated self-deprecation from a supposed communications professional.
jokes.explanation: Mike's defeated acceptance of his own incompetence is sad and funny. 'I think I might have heard of that' implies he knows what a press release is at the same level he knows about the moon landing — theoretically, from a distance.

jokes.text: 'Cause I think that I signed my name. / What? / Not Selina's name, my name.
jokes.setup: Gary suddenly realizes a catastrophic error — he signed the condolence card with his own name instead of Selina's.
jokes.punchline: The understated revelation — 'Not Selina's name, my name' — delivered with Gary's specific brand of apologetic panic, is the start of a cascading disaster.
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep farce engine — a small mistake with enormous consequence. The clarification 'Not Selina's name, my name' is delivered as if Gary hopes the distinction won't matter, which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: It's gonna look like the VEEP couldn't be bothered to sign a condolence card for one of the most respected perverts in the Senate.
jokes.setup: The team realizes the political fallout of Gary signing his own name on the card.
jokes.punchline: 'Most respected perverts in the Senate' — the casual normalization of senatorial sexual harassment as a category of esteem.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding line. 'Most respected perverts in the Senate' is the satirical thesis of the whole scene — Washington's institutional respect for terrible people captured in five words. The callback to Reeves' sexual harassment ('Rapey Reeves,' grabbing Selina's 'left tit') makes it a well-earned joke. The word 'respected' doing double duty — both sincere (he was politically respected) and ironic (he was a pervert) — is excellent craft.

jokes.text: If I were writing a memoir, which I'm not, but if I were, whew, this would be a big moment.
jokes.setup: The team processes the compounding disasters of the day.
jokes.punchline: Selina's memoir-hypothetical aside reveals that even in crisis she's performatively narrating her own story — the ultimate narcissist's reflex.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment. The self-conscious framing of a disaster as memoir material — the denial ('which I'm not') followed by the resumption — is pitch-perfect Selina narcissism. She can't stop thinking of herself as the protagonist of an important story even when she's in the middle of causing a stupid crisis.

jokes.text: Gary's dramatic return to confess the signed card — the extended silence after 'So sorry. I just need to tell you something.' [21:25 to 21:40]
jokes.setup: Gary storms back into what appears to be Selina's meeting to confess about the signed card.
jokes.punchline: The 15-second gap between his entrance and 'What the fuck, Amy?' suggests a prolonged, horrified pause as Gary tries to deliver bad news in the worst possible way.
jokes.explanation: A performance-dependent cringe beat that relies on the physical comedy of Gary's interruption. The timestamp gap suggests extended agonized silence. The structural joke is about Gary's inability to contain the crisis — he makes everything worse by trying to help.

jokes.text: What the fuck, Amy? / I am putting out two fires in there, and I turn around to find out that you have set fire to the fucking fire truck.
jokes.setup: Amy confronts Gary about barging in to confess about the card.
jokes.punchline: 'You have set fire to the fucking fire truck' — Amy's metaphor captures perfectly the recursive stupidity of Gary making the solution into a new problem.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. 'Setting fire to the fucking fire truck' is the perfect recursive disaster metaphor — not just making things worse, but destroying the very mechanism of rescue. The construction is original (this isn't a stock phrase) and it's economically delivered with Amy's signature clipped fury. One of the most quotable lines in the episode.

jokes.text: The level of incompetence in this office is staggering.
jokes.setup: Selina emerges to discover the cascading card disaster that her staff has created.
jokes.punchline: Selina assessing her office's incompetence — while she herself is the source of most of the day's crises — is a perfect irony.
jokes.explanation: A solid irony beat — the boss blaming the staff for the boss's own crisis. Not especially original as a beat but works in context.

jokes.text: Fucktard.
jokes.setup: Selina is venting about the mental health charity representative (Burton) she has to apologize to.
jokes.punchline: She uses the compound word 'fucktard' — combining two of the exact type of language she's in trouble for — while in the middle of apologizing for that language.
jokes.explanation: A single-word joke that is perhaps the episode's most structurally perfect comedic moment. Selina is in the middle of apologizing to a mental health charity for using 'retard' — and she uses 'fucktard' (combining fuck + retard) to describe that same charity's representative. The character integration is exceptional: she literally cannot stop doing the thing she's apologizing for. The fact that the word appears alone on a transcript line — followed by 'God!' and 'Okay. It's fine.' — suggests it lands with maximum impact. This is Veep at its thematic best.

jokes.text: Yeah, and of course your happiness is top of my agenda, right above climate control and child trafficking.
jokes.setup: Mike says he's not happy about having to give the press briefing.
jokes.punchline: Amy places Mike's happiness on an agenda above climate control and child trafficking — treating all three as equivalent priorities.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Amy line. The choice of 'climate control and child trafficking' as the two higher priorities is inspired — they're not random grave issues, they're a climate concern and a human atrocity, placed in sequence as if of equal weight. The escalation from environmental to human rights abuse, with Mike's feelings above both, is perfect. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Oh, my gosh, is this gonna become like 'Moonlighting' where we fall in love and start fucking?
jokes.setup: Amy makes a sharp comment to Mike in a context where they're at odds.
jokes.punchline: Mike breaks the tension by invoking 'Moonlighting' — suggesting their antagonism might be sexual tension — which is completely bizarre.
jokes.explanation: Mild joke that works mainly on Mike's register — his social awkwardness producing an absurd pop culture reference. 'Moonlighting' is dated enough to be funny coming from Mike. The 'where we fall in love and start fucking' extension is more blunt than sharp.

jokes.text: First of all, you should put the hood down. / I wasn't going to put the hood up. / Well, good luck, Gary. / I am convinced that you can probably do this.
jokes.setup: Gary is being sent on the covert mission to retrieve the incorrectly-signed card from Jonah's office.
jokes.punchline: Amy's send-off — 'I am convinced that you can probably do this' — is a spectacularly backhanded encouragement.
jokes.explanation: 'I am convinced that you can probably do this' — the hedged sincerity of 'probably' undercuts the encouragement completely. The hood joke is a small character beat establishing Gary's anxiety. Together the exchange is a fine Gary farewell.

jokes.text: Are you here to steal the incorrectly-signed card? / Come on, no. / This looks really bad. / It's not bad.
jokes.setup: Gary arrives at Jonah's office on his covert retrieval mission.
jokes.punchline: Jonah immediately guesses exactly why Gary is there — Gary's denial is transparent and Jonah has complete power in this moment.
jokes.explanation: Good cringe setup. Jonah's immediate, accurate read of Gary's mission makes Gary's denial pathetic. The role reversal — Gary normally more powerful than Jonah in the office hierarchy — is enjoyable.

jokes.text: I got acid reflux. Let's get to the bad part.
jokes.setup: Gary and Jonah are circling around what Jonah wants in exchange for the card.
jokes.punchline: Gary's admission of acid reflux as the reason to speed to the bad news is an absurdly specific and sympathetic detail.
jokes.explanation: A precise character detail — Gary's body manifesting his anxiety as acid reflux is very specific and humanizing. The 'Let's get to the bad part' acceptance is resigned and perfectly Gary.

jokes.text: I want Amy to go on a date with me tonight. / Okay, okay, okay. That's impossible. / Don't worry about it, okay? We're not gonna have sex, all right? Because she hates me. / Okay. Uh-huh. / But Amy's an eight. / Okay. / And if all the other eights that I do want to have sex with see that I am eight-capable, then I'm going to be having sex with an eight very soon.
jokes.setup: Jonah reveals his price for returning the card: a date with Amy.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's 'eight-capable' dating strategy — using Amy as a proof of concept for women he actually wants — is spectacularly delusional and specific.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest Jonah jokes. The 'eight-capable' theory is a detailed, internally consistent, and completely insane social strategy. The structure of the argument — 'she hates me, she's an eight, seeing me with an eight makes me eight-capable' — is a perfect piece of absurdist logic. Jonah's complete sincerity makes it funnier. Gary's 'okay' responses throughout show his gradual horrified acceptance. Very quotable.

jokes.text: It is intern season, Gary. / Do I look like a pimp to you? / You look exactly like a pimp.
jokes.setup: Jonah tells Gary it's 'intern season' as justification for his dating strategy.
jokes.punchline: Gary's 'Do I look like a pimp?' is meant to be rhetorical; Jonah's flat 'You look exactly like a pimp' is the punchline.
jokes.explanation: The reversal of expected answer is perfect — Gary's rhetorical question is answered with complete conviction. 'You look exactly like a pimp' applied to Gary (who carries 'The Leviathan' and fusses over Selina's coat) is both wrong and somehow right. Clean, economical, very funny.

jokes.text: For me, that was 12 years of marriage.
jokes.setup: Gary reports that Jonah 'just wants dinner and a date and no sex.'
jokes.punchline: Selina identifies the no-sex dinner as equivalent to her 12-year marriage — a bleak and very funny self-revelation.
jokes.explanation: Devastating and economical. Selina casually reveals 12 years of loveless marriage in six words, tied to a joke about Jonah's date conditions. The specificity of '12 years' (not 'my marriage' — 12 years) makes it land harder. Excellent character integration: only Selina would reveal this in this context.

jokes.text: Well, that's a capital offense. / Yeah, you could be legally electrocuted for doing that, just so you know. / I'm not here. I have plausible deniability.
jokes.setup: The team realizes they need to forge the President's signature on the new card.
jokes.punchline: The casual discussion of the legal penalty for forging the president's signature, followed by Selina's 'I'm not here' plausible deniability, is very funny.
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy beat about the casual lawbreaking of the VP's office. 'Legally electrocuted' is the precise detail that works. Selina's plausible-deniability reflex (callback to her 'I don't want to know' motto) is consistent characterization.

jokes.text: The key is you do it upside down. / That is extraordinary. / Is there anything you can't do? / Foreplay, direct sunlight.
jokes.setup: Selina reveals her technique for forging signatures (upside down).
jokes.punchline: Amy asks if there's anything she can't do. Selina answers with 'Foreplay, direct sunlight' — two deeply personal revelations delivered as a two-item list.
jokes.explanation: One of the single best jokes in the episode. 'Foreplay, direct sunlight' is a perfect two-item list — the two things are unrelated, both personal, and together they paint a complete picture of Selina's life without a word of explanation. The economy is flawless: four words that reveal she's in a loveless marriage and either a vampire or someone who never leaves the office. The rhythm of 'foreplay, direct sunlight' as a deadpan list is exceptional. Strong comparison to the '8.0 anchor' reference — this clears it.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, well, I'm fluent in bastard. Okay? It's one of my languages.
jokes.setup: Amy protests Selina hiring Dan, the 'biggest bastard in D.C.'
jokes.punchline: 'I'm fluent in bastard' — framing political ruthlessness as a linguistic competency.
jokes.explanation: Excellent original metaphor. 'Fluent in bastard' is the kind of coinage that defines a character in a phrase — Selina's self-aware toughness, her transactional world view, and her somewhat performative cynicism are all in those three words. 'It's one of my languages' is the perfect follow-up. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Amy, I used Dan to get what I wanted. He used you to get what he wanted. Uh-uh, no. I definitely used him. I'm the user, and he's the usee.
jokes.setup: Amy suggests Dan manipulated Selina into hiring him.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm the user, and he's the usee' — Selina coins a grammatically dubious term to insist on her own agency.
jokes.explanation: 'The usee' is another Selina word-coinage in an episode full of them ('misjoked,' etc.). It works because the grammar is consistent — if there's a user, there's a usee — but the insistence reveals she's protesting too much. The Amy-knows-better context makes it funnier.

jokes.text: In six months, when all this bullshit dies down, we're gonna put an oil guy on the clean jobs commission. / You're pouring oil on clean jobs? / Oh, please, please, Amy, grow up. / This is how they solved the Cuban missile crisis. / This is my Cuba.
jokes.setup: Selina explains her strategy: put an oil industry insider on her clean jobs commission to appease plastics.
jokes.punchline: 'This is how they solved the Cuban missile crisis. This is my Cuba.' — Selina compares appeasing the plastics lobby to Kennedy's nuclear brinkmanship.
jokes.explanation: 'This is my Cuba' is a great button — Selina's grandiosity perfectly applied to a trivial political compromise. The parallel structure of Cuban missile crisis / plastics lobby is absurd and also reveals exactly how Selina self-mythologizes. 'You're pouring oil on clean jobs' is a nice punchy observation from Amy.

jokes.text: He's shitty me, okay? I need a shit.
jokes.setup: Amy says Dan is a shit; Selina agrees but insists she hired him for exactly that reason.
jokes.punchline: 'He's shitting me' as a gerund: 'He's shitty me' — a grammatically impossible but perfectly intuitive construction meaning he's being a shit for/toward me.
jokes.explanation: Another Selina verbal construction — this time applying the Mike speech's vocabulary ('shit') back into her own usage as a gerund. 'He's shitty me' is a slightly messy construction but funny for its specificity. 'I need a shit' landing as the final appeal is a good button.

jokes.text: Well, I hope that's one of our cellulose flags.
jokes.setup: Selina proudly shows Jeffries of the Plastics and Cellulose Association her personal Vice-Presidential flag.
jokes.punchline: Jeffries immediately pivots to asking if it's made of his industry's products — he is incapable of dropping the commercial angle even in a moment of diplomatic pleasantry.
jokes.explanation: A sharp character introduction joke — Jeffries' entire personality is lobbying, and he can't separate it from basic social interaction. 'Oh, you're too much' from Selina is a nice straight-woman button.

jokes.text: So, yeah, grab your coat. / Okay, little Jack Horner, there you are. / You got a brand-new desk in the corner.
jokes.setup: Dan is moving into a new desk in the VP's office.
jokes.punchline: Dan greets his new corner desk with a nursery rhyme reference — 'little Jack Horner, there you are' — applied to himself as a self-congratulatory moment of arrival.
jokes.explanation: A character beat showing Dan's self-mythologizing — he's Jack Horner 'putting in his thumb and pulling out a plum.' The nursery rhyme application is clever if not particularly sharp. Works as a final Dan character statement.

jokes.text: Actually, she's not my girlfriend anymore. I just broke up with her about 15 minutes ago via text. / Well, cold and rather nerdy. / Actually, it was an app.
jokes.setup: Gary makes a joke about Dan displaying a photo of his girlfriend Carol Hallowes at his new desk.
jokes.punchline: Dan has already broken up with her via text. Gary calls it 'cold and rather nerdy.' Dan corrects: 'Actually, it was an app.' — the correction makes it worse.
jokes.explanation: Excellent three-beat joke with a superb button. 'Via text' is already cold; 'cold and rather nerdy' is Gary's perfect response; 'Actually, it was an app' escalates the dehumanization to an even worse register. Dan correcting the medium of his cruelty — not defending the act, just specifying the technology — is his character completely exposed. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Touch me and you lose a finger. / And a ball.
jokes.setup: Dan settles into his new desk near Amy.
jokes.punchline: Amy warns Dan not to touch her; Dan escalates the threat to include castration.
jokes.explanation: Short and punchy. Amy's territorial threat is clean; Dan's castration escalation is the right counter-move — it raises the stakes while establishing the antagonistic dynamic. The economy is excellent.

tmdb_overview: Vice President Selina Meyer and her staff - chief of staff Amy, spokesperson Mike, right-hand man Gary, and executive assistant Sue - are still finding their footing in Washington D.C. Selina decides to make the Clean Jobs Commission a pet project, but a series of missteps requires her staff to work damage control.
insight: Pilot establishes Veep's cringe baseline with 89 jokes across 44 minutes—setup for nastier seasons ahead.
